name: Arborist.Framework
comment:
Arborist is a test framework for asynchronous code.

It is intended for use mostly with Tasks. Tests are defined as assertions on
tasks, which are executed in parallel and reported on the command line.

# Declaring tests
@docs Test, Tests, Name, test

# Assertions
@docs assert, pass, fail, failWith

unions.name: Test
unions.comment: A test case, usually constructed with the `test` function.

values.name: assert
values.comment:
`assert` runs a matcher against a value. All values are generally wrapped in tasks.

    let
      a = Task.succeed 7
      b = Task.succeed 3
      c = Task.map2 (+) a b
    in
      assert c (equals (Task.succeed 10))

This function is re-exported from `Arborist.Assertions` for your convenience.
values.type: Task a b -> Matcher a b -> Assertion

values.name: fail
values.comment:
`fail` forces the test to fail.

This function is re-exported from `Arborist.Assertions` for your convenience.

values.name: failWith
values.comment:
`failWith` forces the test to fail with a reason.

This function is re-exported from `Arborist.Assertions` for your convenience.

values.name: pass
values.comment:
`pass` always passes.

This function is re-exported from `Arborist.Assertions` for your convenience.

values.name: test
values.comment:
Defines a test case.

    test "One plus one is most definitely two" (
      assert (Task.succeed (1 + 1)) (equals (Task.succeed 2))
    )

name: Arborist.Matchers

aliases.name: Matcher
aliases.comment: A matcher is a function that accepts a value and asserts some property about it.

values.name: equals
values.comment:
Asserts that two values are equal.

    assert (Task.succeed "Hello!") (equals (Task.succeed "Hello!"))
values.type: Task a b -> Matcher a b

values.name: fails
values.comment:
Asserts that another assertion fails.

    assert (assert (Task.succeed 1) (equals (Task.succeed 2))) fails

values.name: failsWith
values.comment:
Asserts that another assertion fails with specific failure messages.

    assert (assert (Task.succeed True) (equals (Task.succeed False))) failsWith [
      ("Expected", False),
      ("Actual", True)
    ]

values.name: isIntBetween
values.comment:
Asserts an integer is between two others, inclusive.

    assert (Task.succeed 24) (isIntBetween (Task.succeed 12) (Task.succeed 36))
values.type: Task a Int -> Task a Int -> Matcher a Int

values.name: not'
values.type: Matcher a b -> Matcher a b

values.name: onFailure
values.comment:
When constructing a matcher, adds failure messages.

If the matching operation results in an error, the error message is included in
the failure messages as the first item, with a name of "Error".

This function is often used with `sequenceMessages`.

    equals : Task a b -> Matcher a b
    equals expected actual =
      Task.map2 (==) expected actual |> onFailure (sequenceMessages [
        ("Expected", expected),
        ("Actual", actual)
      ])
values.type: Task a FailureMessages -> Task a Bool -> Assertion

values.name: sequenceMessages
values.comment:
Converts a list of failure messages which have value tasks into a task of failure messages.

    let
      a = Task.succeed 1
      b = Task.succeed 2
    in
      assert (sequenceMessages [("A", a), ("B", b)]) (equals (Task.succeed [("A", 1), ("B", 2)]))
values.type: List ( String, Task a b ) -> Task never FailureMessages

name: Arborist.Assertions
comment:
Provide a mechanism for asserting that a value (wrapped in a task)
successfully matches the behaviour outlined by the matcher.

# Assertions

@docs assert, pass, fail, failWith

# Types

The types provided are generally to be used when constructing your own matchers.

@docs Assertion, FailureMessage, FailureMessages

aliases.name: Assertion
aliases.comment: `Assertion` is a task representing either success, failure or error.

aliases.name: FailureMessage
aliases.comment: A `FailureMessage` consists of a name and a value to be displayed on failure.

values.name: assert
values.comment:
`assert` runs a matcher against a value. All values are generally wrapped in tasks.

    let
      a = Task.succeed 7
      b = Task.succeed 3
      c = Task.map2 (+) a b
    in
      assert c (equals (Task.succeed 10))
values.type: Task a b -> (Task a b -> Assertion) -> Assertion

values.name: failWith
values.comment: `failWith` forces the test to fail with a reason.
